preFix.title: Candling eggs during incubation
preFix.subtitle: Using a light source to check which eggs are developing and which are clear or dead.
preFix.excerpt: Candling lets you see inside an incubating egg without opening it. A strong light shone through the shell reveals whether a chick embryo is developing normally, whether the egg was fertile, or whether it died part way through. This tutorial covers when to candle, what you are looking for at each stage, and how to remove eggs that won't hatch.
preFix.sourceNotes: Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive, chapters on artificial incubation. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens (2018). Cornell Small Farms incubation guide.

content.text: Candling

is the non-destructive window into an incubating egg. A bright light shone through the shell in a dark room shows whether development is on track, whether the egg was fertile at all, or whether the embryo died at some point after the first few days. Removing clear eggs or dead embryos reduces the risk of a bad egg exploding in the incubator and contaminating the rest.
What you need

A bright focused LED torch or a purpose-made egg candler. A dark room or a cupboard. The egg, handled gently. Dark-shelled eggs (Marans, Welsummers) are harder to see through; you need a brighter light and more practice with those breeds.
When to candle
Day 7 (first candle): check for fertility and early development. The earliest you can reliably distinguish fertile from
clear
eggs.
Day 14 (second candle): the embryo is now large enough to see movement. Air cell size is a useful humidity indicator.
Day 18 (lockdown candle): the last check before the incubator goes into lockdown mode. Remove any late dead in shell.
What you see at day 7
Fertile and developing: a dark mass (the embryo) visible near one end, with a radiating network of red blood vessels around it. At day 7 this looks like a small spider sitting inside the egg.
Clear: the yolk floats freely and the interior looks uniformly pale yellow or orange. No vessels, no dark mass. Remove.
Blood ring: a ring of red with no radiating vessels and no embryo mass. The embryo started but died in the first few days. Remove.
What you see at day 14

By day 14 the embryo fills most of the egg. When you hold the large end to the light, you see a large dark area with movement; the chick will shift when the egg is gently rotated. The air cell at the large end has grown; at correct humidity it covers roughly a third of the egg.
A
blood ring
at day 14 is a late death. Remove it promptly; a decomposing egg can rupture and contaminate healthy eggs.
Handling during candling

Keep eggs out of the incubator for no more than five to ten minutes total. Work quickly in a warm room if possible. Hold the egg with the large end up and tilt it so the light catches the air cell. Never shake the egg; the chalazae that hold the yolk in position can be broken by rough handling.

If you are unsure whether an egg is dead or alive at day 14, return it to the incubator rather than discarding it. Check again in two days. A late dead in shell that is not removed may still hatch fine 20% of the time; the cost of keeping it is low.
Air cell size and humidity

Eggs lose water through incubation. If humidity is too low, the air cell grows too fast and the chick has too little fluid at hatch; too high, and the chick drowns in excess fluid. At day 7 the air cell should cover roughly one quarter of the egg; at day 14, one third. If air cells are significantly smaller than this, reduce incubator humidity slightly. If significantly larger, increase it.
